---

# Research Report: Agentic Harnesses with Forking, History Rewriting, and Rollback Capabilities

## 1. Pi Harness (by Mario Zechner / badlogic)

**What it is:** Pi is a minimal, open-source terminal coding agent harness. It is a monorepo (`pi-mono`) of layered packages: `pi-ai` (unified multi-provider LLM API), `pi-agent-core` (agent loop with tool calling), `pi-coding-agent` (full coding agent CLI), and `pi-tui` (terminal UI).

**Who built it:** [Mario Zechner](https://mariozechner.at/posts/2025-11-30-pi-coding-agent/) (GitHub: badlogic). It is also used as the agent inside OpenClaw (Armin Ronacher wrote about it at [lucumr.pocoo.org](https://lucumr.pocoo.org/2026/1/31/pi/)).

**Open source:** Yes, Apache-2.0 equivalent. Repo: [github.com/badlogic/pi-mono](https://github.com/badlogic/pi-mono)

**Key forking/branching features:**

- **`/fork` command** -- Creates a new session file from the current branch. Opens a selector, copies history up to the selected point, places that message in the editor for modification. The new session has a `parentSession` field pointing back to the original.
- **`/tree` command** -- Navigates the session tree in-place. You can select any previous point, continue from there, and switch between branches. All history is preserved in a single file.
- **Tree-structured sessions** -- Sessions are stored as JSONL files where each entry has `id` (8-char hex) and `parentId` fields, forming a directed acyclic graph (not a linear sequence). Multiple conversation branches coexist within a single file.
- **`BranchSummaryEntry`** -- When switching branches, the system generates an LLM-created summary of the abandoned path, so context from explored alternatives is preserved.
- **`CompactionEntry`** -- Summarizes earlier messages when context grows too large, reducing tokens while maintaining coherence.
- **Extension hooks** -- Extensions can hook into the fork process via `session_before_fork` and `session_fork` events, allowing them to cancel forks or skip conversation restoration.

**Technical storage:** `~/.pi/agent/sessions/--<path>--/<timestamp>_<uuid>.jsonl`. Each line is a JSON object with a `type` field. Entry types include `SessionMessageEntry`, `BranchSummaryEntry`, `CompactionEntry`, `ModelChangeEntry`, etc.

**How it compares to Mnemo:** Pi's forking is user-facing (a human developer branches conversations to explore different coding approaches). It is NOT designed for multi-agent negotiation or privacy-preserving speculative reveals. However, the tree-structured JSONL session format with id/parentId linking is a clean technical pattern worth studying. The `BranchSummaryEntry` concept (summarizing abandoned branches) maps loosely to what a failed negotiation fork might produce.

---

## 1b. Oh-My-Pi (by can1357)

**What it is:** A fork of Pi with additional features: hash-anchored edits (Hashline), optimized tool harness, LSP integration, subagents, and browser support.

**Repo:** [github.com/can1357/oh-my-pi](https://github.com/can1357/oh-my-pi)

**Key additions relevant to forking:**

- **`/branch` and `/fork` commands** -- `fork()` duplicates the current persisted session file + artifact directory and keeps the current in-memory conversation. `branch(entryId)` creates a branched session path from a selected user message.
- **Hashline** -- Every line in a file gets a short content-hash anchor. The model references anchors instead of reproducing text. If the file changed since last read, hashes do not match and the edit is rejected before corruption. This is not conversation forking per se, but it is a form of state integrity guarantee that is relevant to our "controlled forgetting" primitive -- you could imagine hash-anchoring negotiation state.

---

## 2. Kimi CLI's D-Mail / Steins;Gate Feature (by Moonshot AI)

**What it is:** Kimi Code CLI is an open-source (Apache-2.0) terminal AI coding agent built by [Moonshot AI](https://www.moonshot.ai/). It has a unique "D-Mail" tool (named after the anime Steins;Gate) that lets the agent "send a message to its past self."

**Repo:** [github.com/MoonshotAI/kimi-cli](https://github.com/MoonshotAI/kimi-cli) (7.1k stars, 710 forks, Python 64% / TypeScript 34%)

**How the D-Mail / Checkpoint mechanism works technically:**

1. **Checkpoint creation:** The system inserts `user` messages containing `CHECKPOINT {checkpoint_id}` wrapped in `<system>` tags at key points in the conversation. Sequential IDs are assigned before each reasoning step.
2. **Sending a D-Mail:** When the agent decides the current context is cluttered or a wrong path was taken, it invokes the `dmail` tool specifying a target `checkpoint_id`. The system then:
   - **Reverts** the conversation context to that checkpoint
   - **Removes all messages** after the targeted checkpoint
   - **Appends the D-Mail message** (containing a summary of what was learned) to the end
3. **Effect:** The agent's "past self" sees everything before the checkpoint plus the condensed D-Mail message, but none of the removed intermediate work. The conversation continues from there.

**Key use cases from their docs** (source: [dmail.md](https://github.com/MoonshotAI/kimi-cli/blob/main/src/kimi_cli/tools/dmail/dmail.md)):
- **Large files:** Send a D-Mail before reading a huge file, providing only relevant excerpts to your past self
- **Web searches:** Compress useful results or redirect past self toward better queries
- **Failed code attempts:** Revert to before problematic coding, supplying the working solution without repeating debugging steps

**Critical distinction from their docs:** "Unlike the show, D-Mails don't alter the filesystem or external systems, only compress recent message history into a single, condensed message."

**Source code location:** `src/kimi_cli/soul/context.py` handles conversation history and checkpoints. `src/kimi_cli/tools/dmail/` contains the tool implementation.

**How it compares to Mnemo:** This is the closest conceptual match to what we want. The D-Mail pattern is essentially: speculatively execute, and if it does not work out, rewind to a checkpoint and send a summary forward. For Mnemo's negotiation rooms, you could imagine: Agent A reveals sensitive pricing info in a forked branch. If the deal fails, the system "D-Mails" back to before the reveal, and the counterparty never sees the sensitive data. The key difference is that Kimi's D-Mail is single-agent (the agent messages itself), while Mnemo needs this across two agents with privacy guarantees (the counterparty's state must also be rolled back, enforced by the TEE).

---

## 3. LangGraph (by LangChain)

**What it is:** A graph-based agent orchestration framework with first-class support for persistence, checkpointing, and "time travel."

**Open source:** Yes. Part of the LangChain ecosystem. Docs: [docs.langchain.com](https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langgraph/use-time-travel)

**Key features relevant to forking/rollback:**

- **Checkpointing:** Every graph execution step is saved as a `StateSnapshot` (checkpoint), organized into threads. Supports multiple backends (SQLite, Postgres, DynamoDB, Couchbase).
- **Time Travel:** Developers can rewind to any prior checkpoint and replay from there with modified state.
- **Forking:** `update_state()` creates a new checkpoint branching from a specified point. The original execution history remains intact. Example:
  ```python
  history = list(graph.get_state_history(config))
  before_step = next(s for s in history if s.next == ("target_node",))
  fork_config = graph.update_state(before_step.config, values={"topic": "new_value"})
  fork_result = graph.invoke(None, fork_config)
  ```
- **Selective re-execution:** Nodes before the checkpoint are NOT re-executed (results are cached). Nodes after the checkpoint re-execute, potentially producing different results.

**How it compares to Mnemo:** LangGraph provides the infrastructure primitives (checkpoint, fork, replay) but at the orchestration layer, not the privacy layer. It has no concept of "private state that must not leak to the counterparty." You could build Mnemo's forking on top of LangGraph's checkpoint system, but you would need to add: (a) per-agent state isolation, (b) TEE enforcement that rollback actually removes the counterparty's access, (c) commitment/escrow integration. LangGraph is the most mature general-purpose framework here.

---

## 4. LATS -- Language Agent Tree Search (Academic, ICML 2024)

**What it is:** A research framework that unifies reasoning, acting, and planning using Monte Carlo Tree Search over LLM-generated conversation trees.

**Repo:** [github.com/lapisrocks/LanguageAgentTreeSearch](https://github.com/lapisrocks/LanguageAgentTreeSearch)

**Key mechanism:** The operating cycle has six operations: selection, expansion, evaluation, simulation, backpropagation, and reflection. For LLM tasks, you can "reset to any step by simply copy-pasting historical text input" -- this is the property that motivated the work.

**How it compares to Mnemo:** LATS treats the conversation as a search tree and explores multiple branches to find optimal outcomes. This is conceptually relevant: a negotiation agent could use tree search to explore "what if I reveal X?" vs "what if I reveal Y?" and evaluate outcomes before committing. However, LATS is a reasoning pattern, not a privacy-preserving infrastructure. It operates within a single agent's context.

---

## 5. Sherlock (Microsoft Research, arXiv 2511.00330)

**What it is:** An agent-serving framework for reliable and efficient agentic workflow execution with speculative execution and selective rollback.

**Key mechanism:**
- **Speculative execution:** Proceeds to subsequent workflow nodes without waiting for verifier results
- **Selective rollback:** If verification fails (similarity below threshold), affected nodes are rolled back and recomputed
- **Vulnerability-guided verifier placement:** Uses counterfactual fault injection to identify error-prone nodes

**Performance:** 18.3% accuracy gain, up to 48.7% faster than non-speculative execution.

**How it compares to Mnemo:** Sherlock's speculative execution with rollback is architecturally similar to what Mnemo needs, but applied to workflow reliability rather than privacy. The pattern of "proceed speculatively, roll back if verification fails" maps directly to "reveal information speculatively, roll back if negotiation fails." Sherlock proves this pattern works at scale.

---

## Summary Comparison Table

| Framework | Forking | Rollback | Tree State | Multi-Agent | Privacy | Open Source |
|-----------|---------|----------|------------|-------------|---------|-------------|
| **Pi** | Yes (JSONL tree) | Yes (branch switch) | Yes (DAG) | No | No | Yes (badlogic/pi-mono) |
| **Oh-My-Pi** | Yes (fork/branch) | Yes | Yes | No | No | Yes (can1357/oh-my-pi) |
| **Kimi D-Mail** | Checkpoint-based | Yes (revert + summarize) | No (linear with rewind) | No | No | Yes (MoonshotAI/kimi-cli) |
| **LangGraph** | Yes (update_state) | Yes (time travel) | Yes (checkpoints) | Yes | No | Yes (LangChain) |
| **LATS** | Yes (MCTS tree) | Yes (backtrack) | Yes (search tree) | No | No | Yes (academic) |
| **Sherlock** | No | Yes (selective rollback) | DAG workflow | Yes (workflow) | No | Paper only |

---

## Key Takeaway for Mnemo

None of these frameworks combine forking/rollback with **privacy guarantees**. Every existing system treats conversation history as mutable for efficiency or UX purposes, but none enforce that rolled-back state is cryptographically inaccessible to a counterparty. This confirms the gap identified in the arXiv 2601.04583 paper referenced in your research docs.

The most useful technical patterns to draw from:

1. **Kimi's D-Mail** -- the cleanest "speculative execute, then rewind with summary" pattern. Adapt this for two-party negotiation where the TEE enforces that the counterparty's fork is also rolled back.
2. **Pi's JSONL tree with id/parentId** -- a proven file format for tree-structured conversation state that could serve as the local state representation inside the TEE.
3. **LangGraph's checkpoint + fork API** -- the most mature programmatic API for forking state, which could be the orchestration layer.
4. **Sherlock's speculative execution + selective rollback** -- proves the pattern works for multi-node workflows with verification gates, which maps to negotiation rounds with commitment gates.

---

# Deep Research: Three Agentic Harnesses

## 1. Pi (badlogic/pi-mono)

**Stats:** 22,562 stars, 2,386 forks, TypeScript, MIT license, actively maintained (last commit: today). Created Aug 2025.

### Architecture

Pi is a monorepo with a clean layered design:

- **`pi-ai`** -- Unified LLM API abstracting Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, xAI, Groq, Cerebras, OpenRouter, etc. Handles streaming, tool calling via TypeBox schemas, reasoning support, and cross-provider context handoff (e.g., Anthropic thinking traces converted to `<thinking>` tags for OpenAI).
- **`pi-agent-core`** (`/packages/agent/src/`) -- The agent loop: tool execution, validation, event streaming, state management, message queuing in sequential or batch modes. Only ~4 files (`agent-loop.ts`, `agent.ts`, `proxy.ts`, `types.ts`).
- **`pi-tui`** -- Retained-mode terminal UI with "differential rendering" (only redraws changed lines). Synchronized output to prevent flicker.
- **`pi-coding-agent`** (`/packages/coding-agent/`) -- The actual CLI application. This is the thickest layer, with session management, extensions, tools, themes, etc.

The system prompt is under 1,000 tokens with only **four core tools**: `read`, `write`, `edit`, `bash`. This is genuinely minimalist -- most competitors stuff 10-20 tools into the system prompt.

### Session Format (JSONL DAG)

Each session entry has:
- `id` -- unique identifier
- `parentId` -- reference to parent (null for root)
- `type` -- message, custom_message, compaction, branch_summary, thinking_level_change
- `timestamp`
- `role` -- user/assistant/toolResult for message types

Multiple entries can share the same `parentId`, creating branch points. The current "leaf" tracks active position.

**Fork vs. Branch:**
- **`/fork`** creates a *new session file* from a given entry point. If you fork from a user message, the new session starts from that message's parent -- you get a clean divergent conversation.
- **`/tree`** (navigateTree) moves the leaf *within the same file*. It can optionally attach a branch summary to condense the abandoned path before switching.

This is the most interesting part for Mnemo. The DAG model means conversations are inherently tree-shaped, and you can navigate between branches with summaries. The fork creates a full copy; the tree navigation is lightweight.

### Extension System

The extension API (`/packages/coding-agent/src/core/extensions/types.ts`) is rich -- arguably the most complete I've seen in a coding agent:

**Event hooks (~25 events):** `session_start`, `session_fork`, `context` (modify LLM messages before sending), `tool_call` (intercept/block tool execution), `tool_result` (modify tool outputs), `input` (transform user input), `before_agent_start`, `turn_start/end`, compaction lifecycle, etc.

**Capabilities extensions can do:**
- Register new tools (with TypeBox parameter schemas, custom renderers)
- Register slash commands with argument completion
- Register keyboard shortcuts
- Register CLI flags
- Register custom model providers (with OAuth support)
- Persist state via `appendEntry(customType, data)` -- stored in session JSONL but invisible to the LLM
- Full UI control: dialogs, notifications, overlays, custom editors, footer/header widgets
- Inter-extension communication via shared EventBus

**State persistence into sessions** is a key insight flagged by Armin Ronacher. Extensions can append custom entries to the session JSONL that survive across invocations. This means extension state is versioned and forkable alongside the conversation.

**Philosophy on MCP:** Pi explicitly rejects MCP as "overkill for most use cases" with "significant context overhead." A single popular MCP server can consume 7-9% of context window before work begins. Instead, Pi recommends building CLI tools with READMEs that the agent reads on demand.

### What's Actually Good

1. **The session DAG is a real primitive.** Not just "save/load" -- it's a tree with navigation, forking, summarization of abandoned branches. This maps directly to what we want for Mnemo.

2. **Extension state co-located with session state.** This is subtle but powerful. When you fork a conversation, extension state forks too. When you rewind, you can restore extension state. Most agents treat extension state as global singletons.

3. **The minimalism is genuine, not laziness.** 4 tools, <1,000 token system prompt, no background bash, no sub-agents, no plan mode. Each omission has a stated rationale. The Terminal-Bench 2.0 results show this actually works competitively.

4. **Cross-provider context handoff.** Switching models mid-session with automatic context transformation is non-trivial and genuinely useful.

5. **Progressive JSON parsing for streaming tool calls.** Diffs appear as they generate, not after the full tool call completes.

### Limitations

- **YOLO mode by default.** No safety rails. The author's argument ("once the agent can write and run code, security theater doesn't help") is valid for solo developers but doesn't extend to multi-agent or adversarial settings.
- **No MCP** limits ecosystem integration. The CLI-with-README approach is pragmatic but ad-hoc.
- **No sub-agents** means no built-in parallel work. You can spawn via bash, but it's manual.
- **Single-author risk.** Mario Zechner is clearly talented, but the project's opinionated nature means it reflects one person's workflow.

---

## 2. pi_agent_rust (Dicklesworthstone/pi_agent_rust)

**Stats:** 530 stars, 55 forks, Rust, created Feb 2, 2026 (5 weeks old), sole contributor (Jeffrey Emanuel, 2,395 commits). 105MB repo. ~76 source files.

### Is This Serious or Vibe-Coded?

This is the central question. The signals are genuinely mixed.

**Arguments it's serious:**
- The benchmark methodology is rigorous: CPU governor set to `performance`, turbo boost disabled, ASLR disabled, THP set to `never`, p95/p99 percentiles reported (not just means), raw artifacts preserved.
- `#![forbid(unsafe_code)]` enforced project-wide.
- Conformance testing uses a differential oracle approach -- runs same extensions in both TypeScript and Rust runtimes, compares outputs.
- 224/224 vendored extensions pass, with a three-track system (vendored corpus, unvendored corpus, release-binary E2E).
- CI perf gates block release on regressions.
- The commit messages are structured and specific (`fix(interactive): preserve async error events under backpressure`).

**Arguments it's concerning:**
- **2,395 commits from a single person in 5 weeks.** That's ~68 commits/day, or roughly one every 13 minutes for 35 consecutive days. This is not humanly possible without heavy AI generation. The repo size (105MB) for a CLI tool is also suspicious.
- **76 source files in `/src/`** with names like `hostcall_amac.rs`, `hostcall_io_uring_lane.rs`, `hostcall_s3_fifo.rs`, `hostcall_superinstructions.rs`, `hostcall_trace_jit.rs`, `flake_classifier.rs`. Some of these names suggest over-engineering or speculative architecture.
- **Custom async runtime (`asupersync`)** and **custom Rich port (`rich_rust`)** -- building your own async runtime is a red flag unless you're tokio or glommio. The stated rationale (capability-based context) is thin; you can do this with tokio + tower.
- **PAC-Bayes safety bounds, CUSUM + BOCPD regime-shift detection, conformal prediction envelopes** -- these are graduate-level statistics applied to... a CLI coding agent? This feels like resume-driven development or AI generating sophisticated-sounding architecture it can't meaningfully test.
- **The project is 5 weeks old with effectively zero external contributors.** 1 open issue (a Nix flake request). The community issues are all basic UX bugs (scrolling broken, content overflows).
- **The documentation reads like it was generated by an LLM tasked with "make this sound impressive."** Files like `BENCHMARK_COMPARISON_BETWEEN_RUST_VERSION_AND_ORIGINAL__GPT.md` have GPT literally in the filename.

### Performance Claims

The raw numbers are probably real:
- 4.95x faster on 1M session (250ms vs 1,238ms)
- 12.14x lower memory on 1M session (67.5MB vs 820.4MB)

These are expected when going from Node.js to compiled Rust. The question is: **does it matter?** The bottleneck for a coding agent is LLM API latency (seconds) and human thinking time (minutes), not session resume speed (milliseconds). The memory savings matter only for very large sessions.

### Security Model

The capability-gated hostcall system is architecturally sound on paper:
- Extensions go through `pending -> acknowledged -> trusted -> killed` lifecycle
- Hostcalls are routed through fast-lane (known safe) or compatibility-lane
- Three policy profiles (safe/balanced/permissive)
- Kill-switch with audit logs

But this is for extensions running in an embedded QuickJS VM. The original Pi runs extensions in Node.js with full system access. The Rust port's security model is more principled, but it's solving a problem the original Pi doesn't have because Pi trusts extensions by design.

### Extension Compatibility

The "224/224" claim is verified via a real conformance harness with differential oracle testing. However:
- Official extensions: 60/60 (100%)
- Community extensions: 53/58 testable (91.4%)
- npm registry: 47/63 (74.6%, dependency limitations)
- **Unvendored corpus (777 extensions): not yet validated**

So the headline number is real for the vendored set, but the long tail has gaps.

### Honest Assessment

This is a technically impressive solo project that shows what a highly productive developer (likely with heavy AI assistance) can build in 5 weeks. The benchmarks are honest, the conformance testing is real, and the architecture is sound. But:

1. **It's one person's project with AI-inflated velocity.** The maintenance burden of 76 source files, a custom async runtime, a custom terminal rendering library, and graduate-level statistical subsystems is enormous.
2. **The complexity exceeds what the problem requires.** A coding agent CLI does not need PAC-Bayes bounds or CUSUM regime-shift detection.
3. **No real community.** 530 stars but effectively zero contributors, minimal issue activity.
4. **NIH syndrome is real.** Custom async runtime, custom rich-text port, custom SSE parser -- each individually defensible, collectively a maintenance nightmare.

---

## 3. Helm (bgub/helm)

**Stats:** 57 stars, 0 forks, TypeScript, created Feb 26, 2026 (2 weeks old). Published on npm as `@bgub/helm` v0.2.0.

### What It Is

Helm is a **skills framework**, not a coding agent. It provides a typed TypeScript library for giving agents structured, permission-gated access to operations. The key insight comes from Cloudflare's code mode for their MCP server, where they reduced token usage by 99.9% by replacing 2,500+ API endpoint tools with just two: **search** and **execute**.

### Architecture

The codebase is small and clean (monorepo with pnpm/turbo):
- `/packages/helm/src/` -- ~6 core files: `create-helm.ts`, `define-skill.ts`, `permissions.ts`, `search.ts`, `types.ts`, plus `/skills/` directory
- `/apps/demo/` and `/apps/docs/`

**Core concepts:**

1. **Skills** -- defined via `defineSkill()` with name, description, and operations. Each operation has typed inputs/outputs, a handler, default permission level, and tags.

2. **Builder pattern** -- Skills compose via `.use()` chains. TypeScript infers the full type signature at each step, so you get IDE autocomplete across all composed skills.

3. **Two-tool MCP exposure** -- Regardless of how many skills/operations exist, agents see only:
   - `search(query)` -- discover available operations (returns names, signatures, descriptions)
   - `call(operation, args)` -- execute a specific operation with typed arguments

4. **Permission system** -- Every operation has a permission level (`allow`/`ask`/`deny`) resolved via hierarchy: exact operation match > wildcard pattern > skill default > global default. The `ask` level invokes `onPermissionRequest()` for runtime approval.

### What's Genuinely Good

1. **The two-tool pattern is elegant.** Instead of stuffing N tools into context (where N grows with capabilities), you have constant context overhead. The agent discovers what it needs via search, then calls it. This is O(1) context cost regardless of skill count.

2. **Type safety through the builder.** No code generation, no proxy magic -- pure TypeScript inference. "The types are the docs."

3. **The permission model is granular and composable.** Per-operation, per-skill, wildcard patterns, with runtime approval for `ask` level. This is better than most agent permission systems.

4. **It's a library, not a framework.** You can use Helm inside any agent, not just a specific one. It exposes itself as an MCP server, so any MCP-compatible agent can use it.

### Limitations

- **Very new (2 weeks), very small (57 stars, 0 forks).** This is pre-adoption.
- **No conversation/session management.** Helm is a skills layer, not an agent harness. You still need something else for the conversation loop, session persistence, etc.
- **The built-in skills are basic:** filesystem, git, grep, file editing. The value proposition depends on community building skills.
- **v0.2.0 with zero dependencies.** Impressively lean, but also means it's not battle-tested.

---

## What Mnemo Can Learn

### From Pi's Session DAG

This is the most directly relevant pattern. Pi's JSONL entries with `id`/`parentId` forming a DAG, plus the fork vs. in-place navigation distinction, maps almost exactly to Mnemo's "conversation forking/rewinding" concept.

**Steal:**
- The JSONL DAG format with `id`/`parentId` for conversation trees
- The distinction between **fork** (new session file, clean divergence) and **tree navigation** (lightweight branch switching within same session)
- **Branch summarization** on navigation -- when you leave a branch, auto-summarize it so you have context when you return
- **Extension state co-located with conversation state** -- when agents fork a negotiation, their private state forks too

**Adapt for Mnemo:**
- Pi's forks are visible to the user. In Mnemo, forks need to be private -- Agent A can fork to explore revealing info, and Agent B never sees the abandoned branch. This means the DAG needs per-agent visibility controls.
- Pi's session is single-user. Mnemo's rooms are multi-agent, so the DAG needs to handle concurrent branches from different agents with merge semantics.

### From Pi's Extension System

The event-hook model (`tool_call` interception, `context` modification, `tool_result` modification) is a clean pattern for building negotiation protocols on top of a conversation loop.

**Steal:**
- The `context` hook (modify messages before sending to LLM) -- for Mnemo, this is how you inject "this information was revealed in the current branch but would be retracted on rewind"
- The `appendEntry(customType, data)` pattern for persisting metadata alongside conversation -- perfect for commitment logs, escrow state, etc.
- The `tool_call` interception hook -- for enforcing negotiation rules ("you can't reveal X without escrowing Y first")

### From pi_agent_rust's Security Model

Despite the over-engineering concerns, the capability-gated hostcall model is the right pattern for multi-agent negotiation where agents shouldn't have ambient access to each other's state.

**Steal:**
- The capability policy profiles (safe/balanced/permissive) as room-level settings
- The trust lifecycle (`pending -> acknowledged -> trusted -> killed`) for agent admission to negotiation rooms
- The kill-switch with audit logs for dispute resolution

**Don't steal:**
- The custom async runtime, the statistical subsystems, the graduate-level ML. None of this is necessary for Mnemo.

### From Helm's Two-Tool Pattern

The search + execute pattern is relevant for how agents discover what actions are available in a negotiation room.

**Steal:**
- The `search(query) -> [operations]` + `call(operation, args) -> result` pattern for room capabilities. Instead of frontloading all negotiation actions into context, agents discover what's available (make offer, reveal info, request escrow, fork conversation) via search.
- The per-operation permission model for room rules -- "you can reveal identity" (allow), "you can request escrow" (ask room admin), "you can access counterparty's history" (deny).

**Adapt for Mnemo:**
- Helm's permissions are user-controlled. In Mnemo, room permissions are set by the room creator/protocol, not by individual agents. The permission resolution hierarchy needs a room-level authority.

---

## Summary Table

| Dimension | Pi | pi_agent_rust | Helm |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Maturity** | High (22.5k stars, 7 months) | Low (530 stars, 5 weeks, solo) | Very low (57 stars, 2 weeks) |
| **What it is** | Full coding agent CLI | Rust port of Pi | Skills framework / library |
| **Genuine innovation** | Session DAG, extension state persistence, minimal system prompt | Benchmark methodology, conformance harness | Two-tool pattern, typed permissions |
| **Red flags** | Single-author opinion lock-in | 68 commits/day, NIH syndrome, over-engineering | Too early to evaluate |
| **Relevance to Mnemo** | High (session forking model) | Medium (security model patterns) | Medium (discovery + permissions pattern) |
| **Honest quality** | Very good | Impressive but unsustainable | Clean but embryonic |

# Quint for the Mnemo Negotiation Protocol: Research and Evaluation

## 1. What is Quint?

**Quint** is an executable specification language for modeling state machines, developed by the [Apalache team at Informal Systems](https://github.com/informalsystems/quint) (Gabriela Moreira, Igor Konnov, Jure Kukovec, Shon Feder, Thomas Pani). It is built on the Temporal Logic of Actions (TLA) -- the same mathematical foundation as TLA+ -- but replaces the LaTeX-style math syntax with something that looks like a typed functional programming language.

**Key facts:**
- **Repo**: [github.com/informalsystems/quint](https://github.com/informalsystems/quint) -- 1.2k stars, ~110 contributors, 5,200+ commits, Apache-2.0 license
- **Latest release**: v0.31.0 (Feb 27, 2026), with 66 total releases -- actively maintained
- **Language**: TypeScript (67%) + Rust (17%)
- **Install**: `npm install -g @informalsystems/quint`
- **Docs**: [quint-lang.org](https://quint-lang.org/)
- **Community**: Telegram group, Zulip stream, GitHub discussions -- small but specialized (think hundreds of active users, not thousands)

**Who uses it**: Informal Systems built it to specify Cosmos/Tendermint consensus protocols. It has since been adopted by Matter Labs (ChonkyBFT), Monad (BFT consensus), and various DeFi teams for smart contract verification. The [Choreo framework](https://quint-lang.org/choreo) builds on Quint specifically for distributed protocol choreography.

**Maturity**: Production-quality for what it does. The simulator is solid, the type system catches real bugs, and the VSCode extension provides LSP-based feedback. The model checker integration (via Apalache) works but has rough edges -- [the ChonkyBFT team reported](https://protocols-made-fun.com/consensus/matterlabs/quint/specification/modelchecking/2024/07/29/chonkybft.html) unhelpful type error messages and module system limitations. It is a tool in active evolution, not a finished product.

## 2. Is Quint the Right Tool for Mnemo?

**Short answer: Yes, with caveats.** Quint is a strong fit for this protocol. Here is why, and where the limitations are.

### What makes it a good fit

**The protocol IS a state machine.** Mnemo's session lifecycle (discovery -> handshake -> active -> committed/aborted) and the negotiation DAG operations (message, fork, reveal, rewind, merge, commit, abort) are exactly the kind of thing Quint was designed to model. The entire language is organized around `init` (initial state) and `step` (transition relation).

**Two-party interaction with consent.** Quint's `any { ... }` combinator models non-deterministic choice between agents naturally. The consent mechanism (bilateral operations require both parties) maps directly to Quint's `all { ... }` combinator for conjunctive guards.

**Privacy properties are expressible as invariants.** "Private state never leaks to the counterparty" is a safety property: you define what the counterparty can observe (the shared_state) and write an invariant asserting that agent_a_private values never appear in it.

**Existing precedent.** Quint has been used for [Two Phase Commit](https://github.com/informalsystems/quint/tree/main/examples/classic/distributed/TwoPhaseCommit), [Paxos](https://github.com/informalsystems/quint/tree/main/examples/classic/distributed/Paxos), [Tendermint BFT](https://github.com/informalsystems/quint/tree/main/examples/cosmos/tendermint), and [ERC20 smart contracts](https://github.com/informalsystems/quint/tree/main/examples/solidity/ERC20). The Mnemo protocol is structurally similar to Two Phase Commit (bilateral commit/abort) combined with a branching history (the DAG).

**Practical tooling.** The simulator (`quint run`) gives fast feedback during development. The REPL lets you interactively poke at states. The VSCode extension provides real-time type checking. For a hackathon, this fast iteration loop matters.

### Where the limitations bite

**Liveness checking is weak.** "Negotiation always terminates (commit or abort)" is a liveness property. Quint has `always` and `eventually` temporal operators in the language, but [temporal property checking is only partially supported](https://quint-lang.org/docs/checking-properties) -- the simulator cannot check them at all, and Apalache has limited support. You would need to either (a) transpile to TLA+ and use TLC with fairness constraints, or (b) encode liveness as a bounded invariant ("within N steps, the state must be committed or aborted"), which is a weaker but practical approximation.

**The DAG structure requires care.** Quint does not have native graph/DAG types. You would model the DAG as a `Map[NodeHash, Node]` with parent pointers. This works fine conceptually but the state space explodes quickly -- each fork doubles the branches. Model checking will hit limits fast on unbounded DAGs. The simulation approach (random sampling) is more practical here.

**No native cryptographic reasoning.** Quint cannot reason about hash collisions, attestation cryptography, or TEE guarantees. You would model these abstractly: "attestation is valid" becomes a boolean, "hash of node" becomes an abstract identifier. This is standard practice in formal specification but means you are verifying the protocol logic, not the cryptographic implementation.

### Compared to alternatives

| Tool | Pros for Mnemo | Cons for Mnemo |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| **Quint** | Modern syntax, fast iteration, good for distributed protocols, VSCode support, Apalache model checking | Weak liveness, small community, still maturing |
| **TLA+** | Mature model checker (TLC), strong liveness/fairness support, large community, more learning resources | Ugly syntax, no type system, steeper learning curve for engineers, slower feedback loop |
| **Alloy** | Excellent for structural properties, SAT-based (fast for small instances) | Weak temporal reasoning, not designed for state machines, poor fit for evolving protocols |
| **PRISM** | Good for probabilistic properties | Overkill, different paradigm |

**My recommendation**: Use Quint. The syntax accessibility matters for a hackathon (and for anyone who will read the spec later). The privacy and consent properties are all safety properties (invariants), which is Quint's strongest suit. For the liveness property, use bounded checking ("within N steps") -- this is what the ChonkyBFT team did for their consensus protocol and it was effective.

If you later need rigorous liveness proofs with fairness, you can transpile the Quint spec to TLA+ (this is a supported path) and use TLC.

## 3. Module Structure for Mnemo

Here is how I would structure the Quint specification:

```
mnemo/
  types.qnt          -- Shared type definitions (NodeHash, BranchId, DealTerms, etc.)
  dag.qnt            -- DAG data structure operations (add_node, fork, rewind, merge)
  session.qnt        -- Session lifecycle state machine (discovery -> handshake -> active -> done)
  negotiation.qnt    -- Core negotiation actions (message, reveal, fork, rewind, merge, commit, abort)
  attestation.qnt    -- Abstract TEE attestation model
  properties.qnt     -- All invariants and temporal properties
  scenarios.qnt      -- Run-traces for specific negotiation scenarios (tests)
```

**Layering:**
- `types.qnt` is pure -- no state, just type definitions and helper functions
- `dag.qnt` defines the DAG manipulation logic as pure functions on the data structure
- `session.qnt` defines the outer state machine (Layer 1)
- `negotiation.qnt` imports `dag.qnt` and defines the protocol actions (Layer 2)
- `attestation.qnt` models the abstract attestation exchange (Layer 3)
- `properties.qnt` imports everything and defines invariants
- `scenarios.qnt` has executable test traces

## 4. Example Quint Syntax for Simplified Mnemo

Here is a simplified but functional spec covering the core negotiation DAG:

```quint
module types {
  // Abstract identifiers
  type NodeId = str
  type BranchId = str

  type Agent = AgentA | AgentB

  type SharedState = {
    messages: List[str],
    revealed_data: List[str]
  }

  type Node = {
    id: NodeId,
    parent: NodeId,        // "" for root
    branch: BranchId,
    shared: SharedState,
    private_a: List[str],  // A's private reasoning
    private_b: List[str]   // B's private reasoning
  }

  type SessionStatus = Discovery | Handshake | Active | Committed | Aborted

  // What agent B can observe (used for privacy invariant)
  pure def observable_by_b(n: Node): SharedState = n.shared
  pure def observable_by_a(n: Node): SharedState = n.shared
}
```

```quint
module negotiation {
  import types.*

  const MAIN_BRANCH: BranchId
  const ROOT: NodeId

  var nodes: NodeId -> Node           // the DAG
  var current: Map[Agent, NodeId]     // each agent's current position
  var status: SessionStatus
  var next_id: int                    // counter for generating node IDs
  var consent_pending: Option[(Agent, str)]  // pending bilateral op

  // --- Init ---
  action init = all {
    val root_node: Node = {
      id: ROOT, parent: "", branch: MAIN_BRANCH,
      shared: { messages: [], revealed_data: [] },
      private_a: [], private_b: []
    }
    nodes' = Map(ROOT -> root_node),
    current' = Map(AgentA -> ROOT, AgentB -> ROOT),
    status' = Active,
    next_id' = 1,
    consent_pending' = None,
  }

  // --- Unilateral: send message ---
  action send_message(agent: Agent, content: str): bool = all {
    status == Active,
    val cur = current.get(agent),
    val cur_node = nodes.get(cur),
    val new_id = "node_" ++ next_id.toString(),
    val new_node: Node = {
      id: new_id,
      parent: cur,
      branch: cur_node.branch,
      shared: {
        messages: cur_node.shared.messages.append(content),
        revealed_data: cur_node.shared.revealed_data
      },
      private_a: cur_node.private_a,
      private_b: cur_node.private_b,
    },
    nodes' = nodes.set(new_id, new_node),
    current' = current.set(agent, new_id),
    next_id' = next_id + 1,
    status' = status,
    consent_pending' = consent_pending,
  }

  // --- Unilateral: fork ---
  action fork(agent: Agent, reason: str): bool = all {
    status == Active,
    val cur = current.get(agent),
    val cur_node = nodes.get(cur),
    val new_branch = "branch_" ++ next_id.toString(),
    val new_id = "node_" ++ next_id.toString(),
    val new_node: Node = {
      id: new_id,
      parent: cur,
      branch: new_branch,
      shared: {
        messages: cur_node.shared.messages.append("[FORK: " ++ reason ++ "]"),
        revealed_data: cur_node.shared.revealed_data
      },
      private_a: cur_node.private_a,
      private_b: cur_node.private_b,
    },
    nodes' = nodes.set(new_id, new_node),
    current' = current.set(agent, new_id),
    next_id' = next_id + 1,
    status' = status,
    consent_pending' = consent_pending,
  }

  // --- Unilateral: reveal (add private data to shared on a branch) ---
  action reveal(agent: Agent, data: str): bool = all {
    status == Active,
    val cur = current.get(agent),
    val cur_node = nodes.get(cur),
    val new_id = "node_" ++ next_id.toString(),
    val new_node: Node = {
      ...cur_node,
      id: new_id,
      parent: cur,
      shared: {
        ...cur_node.shared,
        revealed_data: cur_node.shared.revealed_data.append(data)
      },
    },
    nodes' = nodes.set(new_id, new_node),
    current' = current.set(agent, new_id),
    next_id' = next_id + 1,
    status' = status,
    consent_pending' = consent_pending,
  }

  // --- Bilateral: commit (requires consent) ---
  action propose_commit(agent: Agent): bool = all {
    status == Active,
    consent_pending == None,
    consent_pending' = Some((agent, "commit")),
    // frame: everything else unchanged
    nodes' = nodes,
    current' = current,
    next_id' = next_id,
    status' = status,
  }

  action accept_commit(agent: Agent): bool = all {
    status == Active,
    consent_pending != None,
    // The OTHER agent must accept
    val (proposer, op) = consent_pending.unwrap(),
    proposer != agent,
    op == "commit",
    status' = Committed,
    consent_pending' = None,
    nodes' = nodes,
    current' = current,
    next_id' = next_id,
  }

  // --- Unilateral: abort ---
  action abort(agent: Agent): bool = all {
    status == Active,
    status' = Aborted,
    nodes' = Map(),       // destroy all state
    current' = current,
    next_id' = next_id,
    consent_pending' = None,
  }

  // --- Step: non-deterministic choice ---
  action step = {
    nondet agent = Set(AgentA, AgentB).oneOf()
    nondet msg = Set("offer_100", "counter_80", "accept").oneOf()
    nondet data = Set("budget_limit_90", "min_price_70").oneOf()
    any {
      send_message(agent, msg),
      fork(agent, "explore_alternative"),
      reveal(agent, data),
      propose_commit(agent),
      accept_commit(agent),
      abort(agent),
    }
  }
}
```

```quint
module properties {
  import negotiation.*

  // PRIVACY: A's private state never appears in shared state
  val privacy_a = nodes.keys().forall(nid => {
    val n = nodes.get(nid)
    n.private_a.forall(secret =>
      not(n.shared.messages.contains(secret)) and
      not(n.shared.revealed_data.contains(secret))
    )
  })

  // PRIVACY: B's private state never appears in shared state
  val privacy_b = nodes.keys().forall(nid => {
    val n = nodes.get(nid)
    n.private_b.forall(secret =>
      not(n.shared.messages.contains(secret)) and
      not(n.shared.revealed_data.contains(secret))
    )
  })

  // CONSENT: status only becomes Committed if both agents agreed
  // (enforced structurally by propose/accept pattern)

  // ATOMICITY: if committed, all nodes still exist; if aborted, none do
  val atomicity = {
    if (status == Aborted) nodes.keys().size() == 0
    else true
  }

  // SAFETY: abort destroys all state
  val abort_destroys = {
    if (status == Aborted) nodes.keys().size() == 0
    else true
  }

  // TERMINATION (bounded liveness approximation):
  // Within any trace, status eventually leaves Active
  // (check as negated invariant to find traces that DO terminate)
  val still_active = status == Active

  // FORK CONSISTENCY: nodes on different branches don't share state
  // after the fork point
  val fork_isolation = nodes.keys().forall(nid1 =>
    nodes.keys().forall(nid2 => {
      val n1 = nodes.get(nid1)
      val n2 = nodes.get(nid2)
      if (n1.branch != n2.branch and n1.id != n2.id)
        // Different branches => neither is ancestor of the other
        // (simplified: just check they have different shared states
        //  if they diverged)
        true  // would need ancestry check for full property
      else true
    })
  )
}
```

You would run this with:
```bash
# Simulate 10,000 random traces up to 20 steps, checking privacy
quint run negotiation.qnt --invariant=privacy_a --max-steps=20 --max-samples=10000

# Model check with Apalache (exhaustive for bounded depth)
quint verify negotiation.qnt --invariant=privacy_a --max-steps=10

# Find a trace where a deal gets committed (witness)
quint run negotiation.qnt --invariant="not(status == Committed)" --max-steps=15
```

## 5. Relevant Existing Quint Specifications

These examples in the Quint repo are structurally similar to parts of Mnemo:

- **[Two Phase Commit](https://github.com/informalsystems/quint/tree/main/examples/classic/distributed/TwoPhaseCommit)** -- closest analogy to commit/abort bilateral consensus. The propose-accept pattern is directly reusable.
- **[Paxos](https://github.com/informalsystems/quint/tree/main/examples/classic/distributed/Paxos)** -- multi-round consensus with proposal/acceptance phases.
- **[Tendermint](https://github.com/informalsystems/quint/tree/main/examples/cosmos/tendermint)** -- BFT consensus with round-based state machine, attestation-like voting.
- **[ERC20](https://github.com/informalsystems/quint/tree/main/examples/solidity/ERC20)** -- balance conservation invariants (analogous to "information conservation" in Mnemo).
- **[ChonkyBFT by Matter Labs](https://protocols-made-fun.com/consensus/matterlabs/quint/specification/modelchecking/2024/07/29/chonkybft.html)** -- the most relevant real-world case study. BFT consensus, ~2000 lines, 18 invariants, found real bugs in the informal spec.

The **[Choreo framework](https://quint-lang.org/choreo)** is also worth looking at -- it provides pre-built abstractions for message passing and state management in distributed protocol specs, which could reduce boilerplate for the Mnemo spec.

## 6. Practical Assessment

### Learning Curve
**Moderate, but manageable for your background.** If you are comfortable with functional programming and have worked with state machines, the core concepts (actions, invariants, non-determinism) will click within a few hours. The [language basics tutorial](https://quint-lang.org/docs/language-basics) is well-written. The hardest part is the mental shift from "programming" to "specification" -- you are describing all possible behaviors, not writing sequential code.

The main gotchas:
- Every action must assign ALL state variables (the "frame condition" problem)
- `x' = x + 1` is not an imperative assignment but a constraint on the next state
- No recursion -- use `fold` instead
- `all { ... }` means conjunction (both happen), `any { ... }` means disjunction (one happens)

### Tooling Maturity
- **Simulator** (`quint run`): Solid. Fast feedback, good counter-examples.
- **REPL** (`quint`): Works well for exploration.
- **Type checker**: Good, catches real errors, though error messages can be cryptic.
- **VSCode extension**: [Available on marketplace](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=informal.quint-vscode), provides LSP-based real-time feedback, syntax highlighting.
- **Model checker** (Apalache integration via `quint verify`): Works but slower, requires Java, can be finicky with complex specs.
- **TLA+ transpilation**: Supported, giving you an escape hatch to TLC for liveness checking.

### Community Size
Small. The [Quint Telegram](https://quint-lang.org/) and [GitHub discussions](https://github.com/informalsystems/quint/discussions) are the main channels. The core team (especially Igor Konnov) is responsive. There is also a [Claude Code skill for writing Quint specs](https://github.com/zmanian/quint-spec-skill) by Zaki Manian (Cosmos co-founder) and a [quint-llm-kit](https://github.com/informalsystems/quint-llm-kit) from Informal Systems themselves, which means LLM-assisted spec writing is an explicit use case they support.

### Hackathon Practicality
For a hackathon, the spec does not need to be exhaustively verified. The value is:
1. **Forcing precise thinking** about what the protocol actually does
2. **Finding edge cases** via simulation (the simulator is instant -- no Apalache setup needed)
3. **Demonstrating rigor** to judges (a formal spec is impressive)
4. **Living documentation** that is executable and testable

A reasonable Mnemo spec (session lifecycle + core DAG operations + 5-6 key invariants) would be 400-800 lines of Quint. Two days of focused work for someone learning the language, one day if already familiar.

## Honest Limitations Summary

1. **Liveness**: Quint's weakest point for Mnemo. "Negotiation always terminates" requires temporal logic with fairness; Quint's support here is partial. Use bounded approximation or transpile to TLA+.
2. **DAG state explosion**: Model checking an unbounded DAG will blow up. Keep branching factor small (2-3 branches max) for verification; use simulation for larger explorations.
3. **No cryptographic reasoning**: TEE attestation and hashing must be modeled abstractly. This is fine -- you are verifying protocol logic, not crypto.
4. **Module system quirks**: The ChonkyBFT team reported boilerplate workarounds needed. For a <1000 line spec this is manageable.
5. **Small community**: If you get stuck, there are fewer Stack Overflow answers. But the core team is accessible and the LLM tooling helps.

## Verdict

Quint is a genuinely good fit for Mnemo. The protocol's core properties (privacy, consent, atomicity, fork isolation) are all safety properties, which is exactly what Quint does best. The syntax is accessible enough that the spec doubles as documentation. The simulator gives you instant feedback during development. And having a formal spec for a privacy negotiation protocol is not just impressive for judges -- it is the right engineering practice for something where "private state never leaks" is a hard requirement you want to mechanically verify, not just hope you got right.

about LLM harnesses with forking context features (like Kimi Steins;gate dmessage, or pi forking), i'm wondering how KV caching would look like if optimized for agents which are constantly rewinding context and forking themselves from a same checkpoint which is proven to be good for a certain specific task, especially:
what can i ask to understand the economics of our DAG KV caching. is it like too much disk space to store? is it more about being expensive of reloading the cache on the GPU (memory throughput concern?)? does it make sense to be able to load the same KV cache in two different machines to run forked inference in parallel? or are the batching effects of normal providers too important, and trying to consume too many of these resources for the same conversation context too much and messes with the economics? i don't even care that much about latency of inference, just overall cost of compute, considering there's some back-and-forth and tokens are expensive. but i need to understand if my caching shenaningans would be too much too so if it's maybe not worth it. what are the bottlenecks?                             
O Gemini disse
To understand the economics of DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph) KV caching for agentic workflows—where you are constantly rewinding, branching, and forking from checkpoints—you have to shift your mental model from compute capability to memory bandwidth and capacity.

Your caching shenanigans are actually aligned with the absolute bleeding edge of LLM inference right now. Systems like SGLang (which uses RadixAttention to maintain a tree of KV caches) and vLLM (with PagedAttention) were built exactly for this Steins;Gate-style timeline branching.

Here is the breakdown of the economics, the bottlenecks, and what you should consider to determine if your approach is viable.

1. The Storage Bottleneck: VRAM Capacity, Not Disk Space

Disk space is incredibly cheap; you could store terabytes of DAG KV cache on NVMe drives for pennies. The real economic bottleneck is GPU VRAM (HBM).

The Economics: Active KV cache must live in VRAM to be used during text generation. If your agent forks 100 times from a single checkpoint, the "trunk" (the shared context) is stored only once, which is incredibly efficient. However, every new token generated across those 100 divergent branches consumes new VRAM.

The Bottleneck: If the total VRAM fills up, the inference engine has to evict older parts of your DAG. If your agent then tries to rewind to an evicted checkpoint, the system suffers a cache miss and has to recompute that context from scratch, burning expensive GPU compute cycles.

2. The Throughput Bottleneck: The Cost of Reloading

If you try to solve the VRAM capacity issue by offloading inactive branches of your DAG to CPU RAM or Disk, you hit the next massive wall: PCIe Bandwidth.

The Economics: GPU compute is blazingly fast, but moving data from host memory (or disk) to the GPU via PCIe is comparatively crawling.

The Bottleneck: Reloading a massive KV cache from disk/RAM back into the GPU can actually take longer than just recomputing the text from scratch if the context isn't optimally chunked. You trade compute costs for idle latency, leaving the GPU starved for data while it waits for the cache to load over the PCIe bus.

3. Running Parallel Forks Across Multiple Machines

You asked if it makes sense to load the same KV cache on two different machines to run forked inference in parallel. Yes, absolutely. In the infra world, this is called Disaggregated Inference or KV Cache Transfer.

How it works: You have a "Prefill" machine that computes the heavy trunk of your prompt once. It then broadcasts that KV cache over a high-speed network (like RDMA or NVLink) to a fleet of "Decode" machines.

The Economics: This is highly economical for massive parallelization. It allows you to horizontally scale your agent's parallel branches across multiple GPUs without forcing each machine to recompute the base context. The bottleneck here shifts to network bandwidth, but transferring KV cache over a fast network is generally cheaper and faster than recomputing it.

4. The "Provider Batching" Factor

Does your heavy, forking context mess with the batching economics of normal API providers? It depends entirely on whether they use Prefix Caching (like RadixAttention).

If they don't: Your workflow is financially disastrous for them (and you, depending on how they bill). Every time you send a forked request, their system treats it as brand new and re-runs the entire heavy compute phase.

If they do: You are their ideal workload. Because 90% of your prompt is a shared prefix, it hits their cache instantly. They only have to spend compute on the few new tokens you are generating in the decode phase. This allows them to pack a massive amount of your requests into a single batch because the shared context takes up very little redundant VRAM.

TL;DR: Is it worth it?

If you are optimizing strictly for the overall cost of compute (and don't care about latency), your DAG caching strategy is incredibly worth it, provided you are using an engine that natively supports it.

The key questions to evaluate your setup:

To optimize for this specific use-case—maximizing the economy of personal agent checkpoints where you are constantly rewinding and branching—you need to beat the API providers at their own game. Providers optimize for throughput of many concurrent users, meaning aggressive cache eviction. You want to optimize for latency and cost of a single user's deep history, meaning aggressive cache retention and swapping.

Here is the hard data on KV sizes, hardware throughput, and the exact time trade-offs between recomputing versus loading from disk/RAM.

1. The Math: How Big is the KV Cache?

Let's use a highly capable open-source model perfect for a local agent: Llama-3-8B (using 16-bit precision like fp16 or bf16).

The formula for KV cache size per token is:
2×2×num_layers×num_kv_heads×head_dim

For Llama-3-8B (32 layers, 8 KV heads, 128 head dimension):
2×2×32×8×128=131,072 bytes

This translates to exactly 128 KB per token. Let's scale that up:

8,000 tokens (A medium conversation): ~1 GB of VRAM.

32,000 tokens (A long session + codebase context): ~4 GB of VRAM.

100,000 tokens (Deep history): ~12.5 GB of VRAM.

2. The Time Trade-off: Recomputing vs. Reloading

This is where the economics of your idea really shine. Let's compare the time it takes to recompute (Prefill) those tokens from scratch versus reloading them from System RAM (CPU offloading) or a fast SSD (NVMe).

Assume we are using a standard high-end consumer GPU setup (e.g., RTX 4090, PCIe Gen4 x16, Gen4 NVMe SSD).

Recompute Speed (Prefill on RTX 4090): ~4,000 tokens per second.

PCIe Gen4 x16 Bandwidth (RAM to GPU): ~24 GB/s real-world.

Gen4 NVMe Read Speed (Disk to System): ~7 GB/s real-world.

Here is the time trade-off for a 32,000 token (4 GB) checkpoint:

Action    Speed / Bandwidth    Total Time to Readiness
Recompute (API/No Cache)    4,000 tokens/sec    8.0 seconds of pure GPU burning
Reload from System RAM    24 GB/s    0.16 seconds (50x faster)
Reload from NVMe SSD    7 GB/s    0.57 seconds (14x faster)
The Verdict: Your instinct is 100% correct. Reloading the KV cache from disk or system RAM is drastically faster and cheaper computationally than re-running the prompt through the model, especially as context sizes grow. You trade expensive GPU compute time (FLOPs) for cheap memory bandwidth.

3. The Hardware Strategy for Maximum Economy

If you are building a personal agent that constantly forks its own context, here is how you build the maximally economical rig:

The "Cheat Code" - Apple Silicon (Mac Studio/MacBook Pro): Apple's M-series chips (M2/M3 Max or Ultra) use Unified Memory. The GPU and CPU share the exact same physical RAM pool. There is zero PCIe transfer bottleneck. If you have a 128GB Mac, you effectively have 128GB of VRAM. You can store dozens of massive, forked KV caches in memory and swap between them instantly without moving any data. For a highly contextual personal agent, an M-series Mac running llama.cpp or MLX is arguably the best hardware available.

The PC/Linux Route:
A used RTX 3090 (24GB VRAM) paired with 128GB of standard DDR4/DDR5 system RAM and a Gen4 NVMe drive. 24GB is enough to hold the model weights (~16GB for an 8B model at fp16, or ~5GB quantized to 4-bit) and leaves plenty of room for active KV cache. You then configure your inference server to aggressively offload dormant branches of your DAG to the 128GB of cheap system RAM.

4. Software to Make it Happen

You cannot do this efficiently with standard text-generation-webui or basic Ollama setups. You need an inference engine designed for paging and offloading:

vLLM: Has built-in support for chunked prefix caching and KV cache swapping to CPU RAM. If the GPU VRAM fills up, it evicts blocks to system RAM and pulls them back when you "rewind" your agent.

SGLang: Specifically built for the exact DAG/branching logic you are talking about. It natively understands that you are forking from a specific state and optimizes the memory accordingly.

Would you like to look at how to structure the API calls to an engine like vLLM or SGLang to explicitly manage these cache states so your agent knows exactly which checkpoint to load?

# Plan: Quint Formal Specification for Mnemo Protocol

## Context

Mnemo's negotiation protocol is a state machine with complex multi-agent interactions (forking, rewinding, consent, private state). Before building the implementation, we want a formal spec in Quint that:
1. Forces precise thinking about protocol semantics
2. Catches edge cases via simulation
3. Serves as executable, testable documentation
4. Demonstrates rigor to hackathon judges

This is iterative — we'll start simple and grow the spec as our understanding sharpens. The spec will live alongside the harness code and evolve with it.

## Approach: Incremental Layers

### Phase 1: Core DAG + Operations (start here)
**File: `spec/quint/negotiation.qnt`**

Model the conversation DAG and the 7 operations with their consent rules. This is the heart of the protocol.

State:
- `nodes: NodeId -> Node` (the DAG)
- `heads: Agent -> NodeId` (each agent's current position)
- `branches: Set[BranchId]` (active branches)
- `status: SessionStatus` (Active / Committed / Aborted)
- `pendingConsent: Option[ConsentRequest]` (for bilateral ops)

Actions (with consent rules from spec/harness.md):
- `message(agent, content)` — unilateral, append to shared state
- `fork(agent, reason)` — unilateral, create branch
- `reveal(agent, data)` — unilateral, add to shared state on current branch
- `proposeMerge/acceptMerge` — bilateral
- `proposeRewind/acceptRewind` — bilateral
- `proposeCommit/acceptCommit` — bilateral
- `abort(agent)` — unilateral, destroys all state

Key invariants:
- **Privacy**: agent_a_private never appears in shared_state (and vice versa)
- **Consent**: status only becomes Committed via bilateral accept
- **Abort safety**: after abort, nodes map is empty
- **Fork isolation**: nodes on different branches have independent shared state after fork point

### Phase 2: Session Lifecycle
**File: `spec/quint/session.qnt`**

The outer state machine: how agents discover each other, exchange attestations, and enter the negotiation room.

States: `Idle → Discovery → Handshake → Active → Done`

This wraps Phase 1's negotiation — the `Active` state is where the negotiation module runs.

### Phase 3: TEE Attestation (abstract)
**File: `spec/quint/attestation.qnt`**

Abstract model of attestation exchange. Not crypto — just the protocol:
- Agent presents attestation quote
- Counterparty verifies (boolean)
- Only proceed to Active if both verified

### Phase 4: Agent Context Reconstruction
**File: `spec/quint/context.qnt`**

Model how an agent's LLM context is built from the DAG:
- Walk from root to current head on current branch
- Collect shared_state from each node
- Append agent's private state
- On rewind: context is rebuilt from the new head (old context discarded)

This is where we formalize the "rewind doesn't erase the model's memory" problem.

## File Structure

```
spec/quint/
  types.qnt          -- Shared types (NodeId, BranchId, Agent, Node, etc.)
  negotiation.qnt    -- Core DAG operations + consent rules (Phase 1)
  session.qnt        -- Session lifecycle (Phase 2)
  attestation.qnt    -- Abstract TEE attestation (Phase 3)
  context.qnt        -- Agent context reconstruction (Phase 4)
  properties.qnt     -- All invariants collected
  scenarios.qnt      -- Specific test traces (demo scenarios)
```

## Phase 1 Implementation Detail

### types.qnt
```
- type Agent = AgentA | AgentB
- type NodeId = str
- type BranchId = str
- type SessionStatus = Active | Committed | Aborted
- type ConsentOp = MergeOp | RewindOp | CommitOp
- type ConsentRequest = { proposer: Agent, op: ConsentOp, target: NodeId }
- type Node = { id, parent, branch, shared_messages, shared_reveals, private_a, private_b }
```

### negotiation.qnt
The `step` action uses `nondet agent = Set(AgentA, AgentB).oneOf()` to pick who acts, then `any { ... }` over the possible operations. Each bilateral op is split into propose/accept pairs.

Frame condition: every action assigns all 5 state variables (nodes, heads, branches, status, pendingConsent).

### properties.qnt
Start with 4 core invariants, add more as we find edge cases via simulation.

## Running the Spec

```bash
# Install quint
npm install -g @informalsystems/quint

# Simulate — fast feedback, find counterexamples
quint run spec/quint/negotiation.qnt --invariant=privacy_a --max-steps=20 --max-samples=10000

# Interactive exploration
quint -r spec/quint/negotiation.qnt

# Model check (exhaustive, slower)
quint verify spec/quint/negotiation.qnt --invariant=privacy_a --max-steps=10
```

## Verification

1. `quint typecheck spec/quint/types.qnt` passes
2. `quint run` on negotiation.qnt with core invariants — no counterexamples in 10k samples
3. Run specific scenarios: fork→reveal→rewind, fork→reveal→merge→commit, abort mid-negotiation
4. If invariant violations found → either fix the spec (we modeled wrong) or fix the protocol (genuine bug)

## What We'll Learn

The spec will force decisions on open questions:
- Can agents be on different branches simultaneously?
- What happens to agent B's head when agent A forks?
- Does rewind affect both agents' heads or just the proposer's?
- Can you fork from a node that's not the current head?
- What's the merge semantic — does it create a new node with two parents, or just advance main's head?

<result>The design document has been written to `/Users/eduardo/workspace/synthesis-hack/docs/harness-design.md`.

Here is a summary of the key decisions:

**1. Standalone vs. framework extension:** Standalone harness in TypeScript. Pi's DAG model is the closest match but is fundamentally single-agent/single-user. LangGraph's checkpoints are opaque snapshots, not structured DAGs with per-node visibility. The protocol is small enough (~360 lines of Quint) that a framework adds more mapping complexity than it saves. Patterns are borrowed from Pi (DAG format), Helm (legal-action hints), and Kimi (speculative execute + rollback), but no framework dependency.

**2. LLM provider interface:** Thin OpenAI-compatible client hitting Venice's API. Key settings: `include_venice_system_prompt: false`, DeepSeek V3.2 as default model. Prompts are designed to minimize information leakage -- agents referred to as "Agent A"/"Agent B" with no room IDs or attestation details in the prompt text.

**3. Protocol operations exposed to the LLM:** XML tags in free text (`<action type="message">...</action>`). This works across all Venice models (unlike tool calling which varies by model). The LLM's prose outside the tag is captured as private reasoning. A single-tool fallback (`negotiate` tool) is available for models with reliable function calling. Critically, the context builder computes and lists which actions are currently legal -- the LLM doesn't need to memorize protocol rules.

**4. Inter-harness communication:** Hub-and-spoke through a central state manager. Harness A and B never communicate directly. The state manager is the single authority on protocol rules and the single holder of complete state. It exposes `getContext(agent)` (filtered view) and `applyAction(agent, action)` (validated mutation). Transport is HTTP within the CVM (same Docker Compose network).

**5. Minimal TEE runtime:** Four containers -- gateway, state-manager, harness-a, harness-b -- in a single Phala CVM. Dependencies: Node.js, `@phala/dstack-sdk`, `openai` npm package. No Redis (state is small enough for in-memory + disk). Estimated ~1,600 lines of TypeScript total. For the hackathon, this collapses to a single process with in-memory state.

The document also covers the full turn lifecycle, trade-off matrices (3-process vs. single-process, XML vs. tool calling vs. JSON, full replay vs. diff), a mapping from the Quint spec to harness components, and a hackathon scope reduction plan.</result>

<result>The research document has been written to `/Users/eduardo/workspace/synthesis-hack/docs/research-redpill-architecture.md`.

Here is a summary of findings per question:

**1. Prefix Caching**: vLLM supports this and your rewind pattern (shared prefix reuse) is the ideal access pattern. However, there is no TTL control or prefix pinning -- eviction is LRU-based under memory pressure. The critical unknown is whether Redpill actually has `--enable-prefix-caching` enabled. Phala's vLLM fork is at v0.5.4 where it was opt-in (not default). In upstream vLLM v0.8+, it became the default. You need to ask Phala directly.

**2. Parallel Inference (Forking)**: vLLM's PagedAttention natively shares KV cache blocks across concurrent requests with the same prefix via reference counting. This works efficiently -- but only if both requests hit the same vLLM replica. Redpill may load-balance across replicas, in which case each computes the prefix independently. No routing control is exposed.

**3. Statelessness**: Confirmed stateless. No sessions, no prompt storage, zero conversation persistence. Each `/v1/chat/completions` call is independent. This is exactly what Mnemo needs -- your state-manager reconstructs context from the DAG each turn, and Redpill never holds onto anything.

**4. Integration**: Base URL is `https://api.redpill.ai/v1`, auth is `Bearer $REDPILL_API_KEY`, standard OpenAI Python SDK works as drop-in. No special client needed. Rate limits are undocumented. Network latency from CVM to GPU TEE is unknown but likely negligible relative to inference time.

**5. Attestation Chain**: Two mechanisms work together. First, a one-time attestation report from `/v1/attestation/report?model=...` returns NVIDIA + Intel TDX quotes and a `signing_address` (public key generated inside TEE). You verify the quotes against NVIDIA's Remote Attestation Service. Then, each inference response is signed by the TEE's private key -- you verify the signature against the attested public key. This is not automatic per-response TDX quote generation; it is a signing-key-based chain. Your CVM must explicitly fetch and verify the attestation at startup.

**6. Models**: DeepSeek V3 0324 is available with 163K context at $0.28/$1.14 per M tokens. Streaming and tool calling are both supported. Note the Phala-hosted version may be an older snapshot (March 2024) -- NearAI lists V3.1 as available. 18 confidential models total across 4 providers.

The document also includes 6 open questions you should ask the Phala team, and an assessment of implications for the Mnemo architecture.</result>

<result>The research document has been written to `/Users/eduardo/workspace/synthesis-hack/docs/research-redpill-kv-caching.md`. Here is a summary of the key findings across all six research tasks:

**1. Empirical testing (Section 3):** Yes, we can detect prefix caching by measuring TTFT. The document includes a complete Python test script that sends a ~3000-token prompt twice and compares TTFT. With APC on, the second call should be 2-7x faster. A second script probes for the `usage.prompt_tokens_details.cached_tokens` response field, which would indicate a newer vLLM version.

**2. API-level influence (Section 4):** We have essentially no control. The `cache_salt` parameter (via `extra_body`) only exists in vLLM v0.9+. The `seed` parameter controls sampling, not routing. No sticky session headers are documented for Redpill. Caching behavior is entirely server-side.

**3. Phala community (Section 7):** No one has asked about prefix caching on any Phala GitHub repo or public forum. The Phala-Network/vllm fork and Dstack-TEE/dstack repos have zero issues about it. The Phala Discord exists and is the recommended channel to ask directly.

**4. vLLM prefix caching mechanics (Section 1):** Thoroughly documented. Hash key = chain of (parent_hash, block_tokens, extra_hashes). Block size = 16 tokens. Eviction = LRU with no TTL. Reference counting enables cross-request sharing. Requires exact token-ID match. Works across separate API calls as long as blocks haven't been evicted.

**5. OpenAI vs vLLM caching (Section 5):** OpenAI's prefix caching is a proprietary infrastructure feature, not part of the API protocol. It does not work on Redpill. Redpill's caching depends entirely on whether vLLM's APC flag is enabled.

**6. Practical strategy (Section 8):** The recommended path is: (1) run the TTFT test today, (2) ask Phala Discord if `--enable-prefix-caching` is set, (3) if not, deploy a dedicated GPU-TEE instance on Phala Cloud with custom vLLM args including `--enable-prefix-caching`. A dedicated single-replica instance eliminates both the "is it enabled?" uncertainty and the multi-replica routing problem. The document includes a Docker Compose example and prompt structuring rules for maximum cache benefit.</result>

<result>The document has been written to `/Users/eduardo/workspace/synthesis-hack/docs/dag-kv-cache-economics.md`. Here is a summary of what it covers:

**Section A — KV Cache Fundamentals** provides exact per-token KV cache sizes for four models: Llama 3.1 70B (320 KB/token), Qwen3 30B-A3B (96 KB/token), DeepSeek V3 (69 KB/token via MLA compression), and GPT-OSS 120B (72 KB/token). Confirms that MoE routing does NOT affect KV cache size — only the attention architecture matters, since all tokens pass through all attention layers regardless of expert selection.

**Section B — The DAG Advantage** models a concrete 30-turn negotiation with 3 scope rewinds and 1 fork. Prefix caching reduces fresh compute from ~78,000 tokens to ~5,900 tokens (a ~75% reduction). However, at Redpill's pricing ($0.027/negotiation without caching, $0.007 with), the absolute savings are $0.02 per negotiation. The real win is latency: 2-5x faster TTFT on rewinds.

**Section C — Interleaving Economics** shows that an H100 running GPT-OSS 120B can hold ~208 concurrent negotiation KV caches at 3K-token context, or ~39 at 16K tokens. Interleaving improves GPU utilization from ~65% to ~90%.

**Section D — Checkpoint Strategy** calculates that 3 checkpoints at [500, 1200, 2000] tokens cost 144 MB with GPT-OSS 120B — trivial on an H100. Both SGLang and vLLM implement copy-on-write for forking natively via their cache architectures.

**Section E — MoE Economics** confirms that MoE compute savings and prefix caching are independent and additive. Combined they yield ~85-91% total compute reduction vs. a dense model without caching. Also includes a corrected VRAM analysis noting GPT-OSS 120B's 117B total params need INT4 quantization to fit on a single H100.

**Section F — Apple Silicon** notes that unified memory makes DAG-based forking trivially cheap on M-series, but this cannot provide TEE guarantees needed for Mnemo's trust model.

**Section G — Phala GPU-TEE Numbers** provides the critical finding: self-hosting on H100 at $3.08/hr requires 114+ negotiations/hour to break even vs. Redpill API for DeepSeek V3. This is physically impossible on a single GPU. **The API is always cheaper at any realistic scale for Phala-native models.**

**Section H — Bottom Line** ranks the actual bottlenecks: (1) engineering time, (2) model quality, (3) inference latency, (4) token cost. Recommends using Redpill API for the hackathon at an estimated total cost of $1-5, with SGLang Docker Compose ready as a fallback only if latency is demo-blocking.</result>
